content:
It’s understandable to be skeptical about compound interest—it can sound like an overhyped idea. Simply put, compound interest means you earn interest not only on your original savings but also on the interest that money has already earned. This differs from simple interest, where you only earn interest on the initial amount.

For example, if you save $1,000 at 5% interest, after one year you’ll have $1,050. With simple interest, you’d earn $50 every year. But with compound interest, the next year’s 5% applies to $1,050, not just $1,000, so your savings grow a bit faster each year.

Over time, this effect becomes more noticeable. The longer your money stays invested, the more your interest earns interest, gradually increasing your total savings. It’s not a quick fix, but with patience, compound interest can steadily help your savings grow.
